metadata.description: Maps where FDA, EMA, and PMDA requirements diverge across key regulatory domains relevant to eCTD submissions

divergences.fda: 90% CI for AUC and Cmax within 80.00-125.00%
divergences.ema: 90% CI for AUC(0-t) and Cmax within 80.00-125.00%
divergences.pmda: 90% CI for AUC and Cmax within 80.00-125.00% (harmonized via ICH)
divergences.notes: Core standard is harmonized across all three jurisdictions

divergences.fda: 90% CI within 90.00-111.11% for AUC and Cmax (reference-scaled, per 2020 guidance)
divergences.ema: 90% CI within 90.00-111.11% for AUC; Cmax also tightened if clinically relevant
divergences.pmda: Case-by-case; no published NTI-specific interval — follows ICH general principles
divergences.notes: FDA and EMA largely aligned on 90-111% interval; Japan lacks explicit NTI guidance, creating uncertainty for global programs

divergences.fda: Reference-scaled average BE for Cmax when intra-subject CV >= 30%; GMR constraint 80-125%
divergences.ema: Scaled average BE with replicate crossover; Cmax widened up to 69.84-143.19%; AUC remains 80-125%
divergences.pmda: No published scaled BE approach; standard 80-125% applied; replicate designs acceptable
divergences.notes: FDA and EMA both allow widened Cmax intervals but use different scaling formulas. Japan does not allow widened intervals, requiring larger sample sizes for HVDPs

divergences.fda: Class I only (high solubility + high permeability + rapid dissolution); Class III more restrictive
divergences.ema: Class I AND Class III accepted (high solubility + very rapid dissolution + similar excipients for Class III)
divergences.pmda: Class I accepted; Class III under case-by-case evaluation
divergences.notes: EMA is most permissive (accepts Class III); FDA is most restrictive; Japan is intermediate. Global programs targeting BCS Class III drugs must conduct in vivo BE for US market even if EMA accepts biowaiver

divergences.fda: US-licensed Reference Listed Drug (RLD) from Orange Book
divergences.ema: EU-authorized innovator (reference medicinal product)
divergences.pmda: Japan-authorized reference product
divergences.notes: Each jurisdiction requires studies against its own reference product. A single global BE study cannot serve all three jurisdictions unless bridging is accepted

divergences.fda: Section 351(k) of PHS Act (BPCIA); FDA can designate interchangeable biosimilars
divergences.ema: Article 10(4) of Directive 2001/83/EC; no formal interchangeability concept at EMA level (left to member states)
divergences.pmda: PAL pathway; biosimilar designation available; interchangeability not formally designated
divergences.notes: FDA is the only jurisdiction with a formal interchangeability designation. EU leaves switching/substitution decisions to member states. Japan allows biosimilar designation but no interchangeability pathway

divergences.fda: Generally 1 year recommended; timing flexible based on product risk profile
divergences.ema: Minimum 1-year pre-authorization follow-up for chronic-use products (binding)
divergences.pmda: Duration based on clinical development plan; no fixed minimum published
divergences.notes: EMA has the most explicit binding requirement for 1-year immunogenicity follow-up. FDA and PMDA are more flexible but practically expect similar duration

divergences.fda: REMS required only if necessary for safe use per FDAAA; not mandatory for all products
divergences.ema: Risk Management Plan (RMP) mandatory at submission for all biologics
divergences.pmda: Risk Management Plan required (aligned with ICH E2E)
divergences.notes: EMA and PMDA mandate RMP for all biologics. FDA only requires REMS if safety concerns warrant it — most biosimilars do not receive REMS

divergences.fda: 25C/60%RH or 30C/65%RH (ICH Zones I/II); 12 months minimum at submission
divergences.ema: 25C/60%RH (Zone II default); 12 months minimum; 30C/65%RH if justified for Zone III/IV
divergences.pmda: 25C/60%RH or 30C/75%RH (Zone II/IVb); Japan is ICH Zone II but accepts Zone IVb data for tropical export markets
divergences.notes: Core ICH Q1A is harmonized but Japan's unique Zone IVb acceptance means stability data generated for tropical markets may need additional conditions for Japan-specific label claims

divergences.fda: Registration required under FD&C Act 510; annual renewal; FDA retains inspection authority
divergences.ema: Manufacturing authorization from member state authority; QP (Qualified Person) release required; GMP certificate from EU authority
divergences.pmda: Accreditation required from MHLW (PAL Art. 13-3); 5-year mandatory renewal; document examination primary (not routine inspection)
divergences.notes: Three fundamentally different approaches: FDA = registration + inspection authority; EMA = member state authorization + QP release; Japan = ministerial accreditation with 5-year renewal. A manufacturer exporting to all three must maintain compliance with all three systems independently

divergences.fda: Major changes = Prior Approval Supplement; Moderate = CBE-30; Minor = Annual Report (21 CFR 314.70/601.12)
divergences.ema: Type IA variation (do and tell, 12 months); Type IB variation (tell and do, 30 days); Type II variation (prior approval); Extensions
divergences.pmda: Notification within 30 days of change (PAL Art. 100); partial change approval for significant changes
divergences.notes: Three different change classification systems with different timelines. A global change requires parallel regulatory strategies for each jurisdiction

divergences.fda: ICH S1 harmonized: 1 long-term rodent study + 1 alternative short/medium-term model
divergences.ema: ICH S1 harmonized: same as FDA
divergences.pmda: ICH S1 adopted: 1 rodent species (rat preferred, 24-30 months) + 1 alternative system; high-dose AUC >= 25x human AUC; limit dose 1,500 mg/kg/day
divergences.notes: Fully harmonized via ICH S1. Japan's formal adoption specifies explicit thresholds consistent with ICH

divergences.fda: 17-section Highlights + Full PI format per 21 CFR 201.56/201.57; Highlights limited to 20 lines for boxed warning; Drug Facts format for OTC per 201.66
divergences.ema: Summary of Product Characteristics (SmPC) per QRD template; 12 sections; Patient Information Leaflet (PIL) required separately
divergences.pmda: Japanese Package Insert (JPI) per MHLW notification format; separate from eCTD labeling; Japanese language required
divergences.notes: Three completely different labeling formats. A single set of labeling cannot serve all three jurisdictions — each requires jurisdiction-specific Module 1 labeling in the eCTD

divergences.fda: Pregnancy and Lactation Labeling Rule (PLLR): narrative risk summary format since 2015; old A/B/C/D/X categories removed for new drugs
divergences.ema: Narrative format in SmPC Section 4.6; no letter categories
divergences.pmda: Pregnancy classification system using unique Japanese categories; additional contraindication requirements for certain teratogenic drugs
divergences.notes: FDA and EMA both use narrative formats but with different structures. Japan maintains its own classification system. Pregnancy labeling must be jurisdiction-specific

divergences.fda: No formal ethnic bridging requirement; accepts foreign clinical data per ICH E5 if applicable to US population
divergences.ema: No formal ethnic bridging requirement; accepts foreign data with justification for relevance to EU population
divergences.pmda: Ethnic factors bridging studies may be required per ICH E5 to extrapolate foreign clinical data to Japanese population; PMDA frequently requires Japan-specific PK/PD and clinical data
divergences.notes: Japan is the primary jurisdiction requiring ethnic bridging studies. Programs relying entirely on non-Japanese clinical data face significant regulatory risk at PMDA

divergences.fda: PREA: required for new active ingredient, indication, dosage form, dosing regimen, route; iPSP due 60 days after EOP2; orphan exemption
divergences.ema: Paediatric Regulation (EC) No 1901/2006: Paediatric Investigation Plan (PIP) required before MAA; approved by PDCO; more granular age-group requirements
divergences.pmda: No mandatory pediatric study law equivalent to PREA/PIP; pediatric development encouraged but driven by clinical need assessment
divergences.notes: EMA has the most prescriptive pediatric requirements (PIP with PDCO approval). FDA requires iPSP with negotiation. Japan has no equivalent mandate — greatest flexibility but also greatest uncertainty

divergences.fda: 7-year market exclusivity; orphan drug tax credit (25% post-TCJA); priority review eligibility
divergences.ema: 10-year market exclusivity (reduced to 6 years if criteria no longer met); protocol assistance fee reductions
divergences.pmda: Re-examination period up to 10 years; 15% R&D tax credit; priority review; development grants available
divergences.notes: Japan offers the longest potential exclusivity (10 years) and highest tax credit (15%). EU offers 10 years but with sunset clause. US offers shortest exclusivity (7 years) but with additional incentive programs

divergences.fda: 15-day alert for serious/unexpected; 7-day for fatal/life-threatening; quarterly for 3 years then annual; 10-year recordkeeping
divergences.ema: 15 days for serious/unexpected (EudraVigilance); PSURs per ICH E2C(R2); RMP updates
divergences.pmda: 15 days for serious/unexpected; 30 days for non-serious; drug use results surveys required for new drugs during re-examination period
divergences.notes: Core 15-day serious/unexpected reporting is harmonized. Japan uniquely requires drug use results surveys (post-marketing surveillance) during re-examination period — no equivalent in FDA or EMA

divergences.fda: eCTD v4.0 mandatory for NDA/BLA/ANDA; electronic format per 21 CFR 314.50/601.14
divergences.ema: eCTD mandatory for centralized procedure; national procedures may accept NeeS or eCTD depending on member state
divergences.pmda: eCTD mandatory for new drug applications; Japan-specific Module 1 (JP Module 1) with CTD-JP format requirements
divergences.notes: All three jurisdictions accept eCTD but Japan requires unique Module 1 format (CTD-JP). Regional administrative sections are jurisdiction-specific in all cases

divergences.fda: 4 programs: Fast Track, Breakthrough Therapy, Accelerated Approval (surrogate endpoint), Priority Review (6 months)
divergences.ema: PRIME (Priority Medicines) designation; Accelerated Assessment (150 days vs 210); Conditional Marketing Authorization; Marketing Authorization Under Exceptional Circumstances
divergences.pmda: Priority Review; Sakigake designation (for innovative drugs developed first in Japan); Conditional Early Approval; Orphan priority
divergences.notes: Each jurisdiction has unique expedited pathway names and criteria. Japan's Sakigake designation uniquely rewards Japan-first development. FDA's Breakthrough Therapy has no exact equivalent in EMA or PMDA

divergences.fda: 5 years for NCE (NDA); 3 years for new clinical investigations; 12 years for biologics (BLA)
divergences.ema: 8 years data exclusivity + 2 years market exclusivity + 1 year pediatric extension = 8+2+1 formula
divergences.pmda: Re-examination period: 8 years for NCE; 4-6 years for new indications; 10 years for orphan drugs
divergences.notes: Biologics data protection diverges most: US 12 years, EU 10 years, Japan 8 years. This affects biosimilar launch timing strategy across jurisdictions

rows: Japan lacks explicit NTI guidance — seek scientific advice
Japan may require larger studies without scaled approach
US restricts Class III; separate BE may be needed for US
US-only interchangeability; EU leaves to member states
RMP mandatory (EMA/PMDA) vs. REMS if needed (FDA)
EMA most prescriptive; Japan most flexible
Japan offers longest exclusivity/highest tax credit
Japan uniquely requires drug use results surveys
12 vs 10 vs 8 years affects biosimilar timing
